exemplar.title: How significant is the role of the media in encouraging voters during the 2024 local election campaign in Prijedor BiH?

detailedExemplarData.title: How significant is the role of the media in encouraging voters during the 2024 local election campaign in Prijedor BiH?

strengths: Overall structure (introduction, analysis, conclusion) is logical and mostly easy to follow.
Language use is generally clear and correct terminology is applied.
Figures and appendices support the narrative.
weaknesses: Long blocks of text and occasional out-of-sequence references impede flow.
Frequent grammatical slips and citation inconsistencies obscure meaning at times.
Dense paragraphs would benefit from subdivision for readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates a basic but imperfect structure and generally clear language, matching criteria for the mid-band. Occasional lapses in coherence and mechanics prevent a higher score, supporting 2 out of 3 marks.

strengths: Research question is concise and clearly frames the studyâs focus.
Project importance is convincingly linked to democratic legitimacy and informed voting.
Engagement activities are explicitly tied to the research question and cover diverse perspectives.
weaknesses: Opening background is too global; needs more Prijedor-specific context.
Claims about party-held media lack concrete citations.
Could expand on why interviews are the optimal method over other approaches.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates precise issue identification and clear contextual explanation, placing it firmly in the top band. The importance of the project is wellâjustified with concrete reasons, and engagement activities are convincingly linked to the research question. Minor gaps (overly generic global framing and missing sources) do not detract from an otherwise outstanding performance, meriting the full 4 marks.

strengths: Interviewee selection is purposeful, covering politician, journalist, and voter.
Bibliography and question lists show some advance consideration of sources.
Secondary turnout data is integrated to inform analysis of engagement.
weaknesses: Logistical details (dates, consent procedures, recording methods) are absent.
No clear timeline or contingency planning for interviews.
Methodology beyond interviews is not sufficiently outlined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student has made a basic plan for research and engagement, and there are occasional links between research findings and engagement activities. However, planning is patchy, lacking detailed logistics and a structured timeline. Performance consistently matches the descriptors for the middle band, justifying 2 out of 3 marks.

strengths: Key concepts (power, legitimacy, interdependence) are applied purposefully in places.
Analysis moves beyond description by comparing media types and regulatory factors.
Stakeholder perspectives are presented and partially woven into the argument.
weaknesses: Course concepts are not consistently defined or referenced with academic sources.
Analysis lacks multilayered depth and critical evaluation of relevance (e.g., attention-span stat).
Synthesis of perspectives is uneven; viewpoints are not fully integrated into a coherent whole.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows adequate application of course concepts and clear, balanced analysis of key factors, situating it in the mid-range of performance. Partial synthesis of stakeholder perspectives likewise supports a moderate score. While there are commendable insights, they are not sustained or rigorous enough for higher bands, warranting 4 out of 8 marks.

strengths: Some critical evaluation of regulatory strengths and limitations is present.
Student cites criteria for judging media fairness.
Limitations of methods are acknowledged in conclusion.
weaknesses: Personal biases are noted but not examined for their impact on analysis.
Reflection on learning is superficial, with little insight into future application.
Recommendations lack actionable detail and stakeholder specificity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation of research and engagement is present but superficial, and awareness of personal bias and reflective learning are minimally addressed. Overall performance aligns with the upper end of the 1â2 band, justifying 2 out of 6 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student states a focused research question â âHow significant is the role of the media in encouraging voters during the 2024 local election campaign in Prijedor, BiH?â â and immediately supplies local context (election year, positions being contested, alleged media ownership by parties).  
â¢ **Issue is explicit and narrow**, centred on one municipality and one electoral cycle.  
â¢ The introductory paragraphs clearly explain why media influence matters to democratic legitimacy in BiH and how traditional vs. social media differ.  
Because the identification is precise and the contextual explanation is clear and relevant, the work fits the top band and merits the upper mark of 4.

Evidence of planning appears in the bibliography and the prepared interview question lists, showing that the student located appropriate primary and secondary sources. However, no timeline, comparative framework or clear methodology beyond interviews is outlined. This reflects a **basic but not well-structured plan**, corresponding to band 2.

The submission specifies who will be interviewed, why anonymity is needed and which levels of analysis will be covered, indicating **some advance consideration of feasibility**. Yet logistics (dates, recording methods, consent, contingency) are not detailed. Planning is therefore present but patchy, fitting band 2.

Key concepts â **power, legitimacy, interdependence, development** â are named and applied to examples (media ownership, youth disengagement). The student also references critical theory and social constructionism. Application is mostly accurate but not sustained throughout every section, so the work sits in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Interview findings are occasionally supported by secondary statistics (youth turnout, attention-span study) and by legal documents on media silence, showing that research **sometimes informs engagement analysis**. The loop is irregular â there is little evidence that early findings shaped later questions â so band 2 is appropriate.

Three engagement activities (interviews with an opposition candidate, a local journalist and a first-time voter) are **directly tied to the research question** and cover actor, institutional and individual viewpoints.  
The submission explains why each interviewee was chosen, what information was sought and how it relates to media influence. That explicit linking of activity to issue meets the descriptor for a convincing explanation, warranting 4 marks.

Language is generally understandable and includes correct terminology, but there are frequent grammatical slips, missing words and citation glitches that **occasionally obscure meaning**. Coherence is therefore uneven, aligning with 2 marks in this strand.

The analysis moves beyond description by comparing the reach of traditional vs. social media, citing turnout data, and exploring how regulation can be manipulated. **Key causal factors and competing viewpoints are considered**, though not exhaustively. This meets the upper level of the 3-4 band, earning 4 marks.

The student acknowledges personal exposure to claims of bias but does not explore how this background might influence questioning, interpretation or conclusions. **Bias is noted rather than examined**, placing the work at the lower end of the 1-2 band.

The essay follows an identifiable structure (introduction, analysis, conclusion, appendix) but long blocks of text and occasional out-of-sequence references create **some confusion**. Thus, it fits the 2-mark description of a basic yet imperfect structure.

Evaluation is present mainly in the concluding section, noting limitations of current regulations and suggesting stronger oversight. Reflection on the **strengths and weaknesses of the studentâs own methods** is minimal, but some criteria for judging media fairness are cited. This superficial assessment aligns with the top of the 1-2 band.

The student justifies the study by linking **media bias to informed voting, legitimacy and development** and also cites a personal motivation (âgrowing up in Prijedorâ amid claims of bias).  
Specific consequences (unfair competition for opposition parties, low youth turnout) show why the project is needed and why interviews are suitable.  
Justification is clear but not richly evidenced throughout the paper, so the strand is placed in the top band but at the lower mark of 3.

Perspectives of politician, journalist and voter are presented and occasionally contrasted (e.g., duty of objectivity vs. perceived bias). Links among views are drawn but not deeply woven into a single argument. The piece therefore shows **partial synthesis** consistent with 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

Reflection is limited to broad statements that the project shows media bias is a worldwide issue and that change is beneficial. There is **little insight into personal learning or future application**, matching the lower half of the 1-2 band.

annotations.comment: Claims about media âholdingâ by parties require concrete evidence or citation; include a source to substantiate this key assertion.
The research question is concise and clearly frames the studyâs focus on media influence during the 2024 Prijedor campaign, providing strong issue identification and contextual relevance.
The student precisely defines the political issue and links it to democratic legitimacy in BiH, demonstrating clear explanation and justification.
Opening with global election trends feels too generic; adding specific local turnout or media examples would strengthen contextual grounding.
The attention-span statistic is interesting but needs critical evaluation of its relevance to Prijedorâs specific voter context.
Incorporate brief demographic data on older vs. younger media consumption to substantiate claims about generational differences.
Linking the research question to both traditional and social media use shows coherent justification of project importance.
Personal motivation is noted but unexamined as a potential bias; the student should reflect on how background influences framing and interpretation.
Key concepts are listed but lack literature references; defining âinterdependenceâ and âdevelopmentâ with academic sources would deepen analysis.
Selecting interviews with a politician, a journalist, and a voter demonstrates purposeful engagement planning covering multiple perspectives.
The inclusion of an individual-level voter perspective enriches the analysis by broadening stakeholder representation.
Connecting Markâs social media strategy to critical theory is an insightful application of course concepts.
The discussion of the media silence rule is descriptive but lacks an example illustrating its impact on campaign messaging.
Aleksandarâs interview insights are strong, but linking each point back to how it âencouragesâ voting would reinforce the studyâs core aim.
The integration of secondary turnout data with interview analysis demonstrates a valuable link between research findings and engagement.
Peterâs description of regulatory constraints provides balanced analysis of institutional factors shaping media coverage.
Recommendations for increased transparency and accountability are valid but require clear, actionable mechanisms to guide implementation.
Proposed fair-representation measures need identification of responsible stakeholders and clear procedural steps.
The call for strengthened oversight should specify which independent bodies would enforce media impartiality and how.
Introducing social constructionism in the conclusion without earlier context undermines coherence; integrate theory consistently.
Global generalization about media bias worldwide duplicates context; tighten focus on Prijedorâs local dynamics to maintain coherence.
Consider focusing the first background paragraph on Prijedor-specific dynamics rather than global election trends.
Define and rigorously reference theoretical concepts like âpowerâ and âlegitimacyâ to strengthen academic grounding.
Engagement plan omits key logistics such as dates, consent procedures, and recording methods; include these details in methodology.
Specify whether interviews were in-person or virtual to clarify methodological approach and ensure reproducibility.
Ensure quoted material from interviews uses consistent quotation marks and exact phrasing for clarity.
Provide a concrete example demonstrating how the media silence rule has been circumvented in Prijedor.
The dense paragraph on social media reach would benefit from subdivision into shorter paragraphs to improve readability.
Link each statistic and claim directly back to the research question to enhance coherence and relevance.

rubricData.name: Global Politics IA: Engagement Activity

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue not identified or irrelevant
2. No meaningful explanation

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: No discussion of why the project matters

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities absent or unrelated to issue

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue named but explanation vague / superficial
2. Limited or missing context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability mentioned but weak or generic

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities listed; relevance implied or weakly linked

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue explicitly stated
2. Clear, precise explanation with relevant context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability clearly justified with specific reasons

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities fully explained and convincingly linked to the issue

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: No link between research and engagement

strands.name: Engagement planning
strands.content: Activities planned but timing / logistics patchy

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: Research consistently informs engagement; findings feed back into further inquiry

strands.name: Depth of political-issue analysis
strands.content: Clear, balanced analysis; key factors explored

strands.name: Synthesis of stakeholder perspectives
strands.content: Effective synthesis; perspectives woven into coherent argument

strands.name: Awareness of personal bias
strands.content: Biases explored; impact on work examined

strands.name: Organization & structure
strands.content: Logical structure; mostly easy to follow
